column1: #This mapping was created following and expanding upon the SSSOM guidance: https://mapping-commons.github.io/sssom/spec/

column16: comment

Both terms have a close meaning, however the field description for the object (MIxS) details more specific recommendations 'concise, unique and consistent within your lab, and as informative as possible. INSDC requires every sample name from a single Submitter to be unique.'. Whereas, the subject, details that the identifier can be identical to Field or Museum ID.

These terms are a close match when the subject 'fieldid' is used for initial portion of the description 'Specimen or sample identifier generated in the field'. If the subject is used as the latter part of the description 'lot number for a bulk collection event.', the subject would be a closer match to 'source_mat_id' for the object.

These terms are a close match when the subject 'fieldid' is used for the latter part of the description 'lot number for a bulk collection event.'. If the subject is used for the initial portion of the description 'Specimen or sample identifier generated in the field' it would be a closer match to 'samp_name' for the object.
The subject can be related to source material identifiers as it can describe the original material collected that will derive sub-sample but it is less similar to the object than fieldid.
Semantically, these terms may have a somewhat related meaning but the expected syntax may be different.

Semantically, these terms may have a somewhat related meaning but the expected syntax may be different.

Both bubject and object expect the name of the institution storing the specimen, as such, semantically, both are exact matches. However, syntactically, if the sample is not available, the object expects string 'sample not available', wheras the subject details no requirement.

Both bubject and object expect the taxonomy identifier for the specimen. However, the object requires it to be detailed as a NCBI ID, whereas the subject expects the ID according to BOLD taxonomic backbone. However, syntactically, if the sample is not available, the object expects string 'sample not available', wheras the subject details no requirement. Syntactically, there is no association as the values will not be related as they belong to two different systems. Syntactic normalization could be possible with a mapping between differnet taxonomic systems, but, even then, the subject would still have a skos:narrowMatch with the object, as MIxS expects both the taxonomic ID and written nomenclature.

Both terms expect the taxonomic identification of the specimen. However, the object expect both the numerical ID and the textual nomenclature, as such, the subject is narrower, as it expect only the textual nomecalture, semantically and syntactically.

Both terms expect the method used for taxonomic classification of the specimien. However, the object contains not only the method, but also, reference databases used, classification rank and thresholds; as such, the subject is narrower, as it expect only the method, semantically and syntactically.

Both terms expect the taxonomy rank of the specimen identification. However, the object contains not only the rank, but also, reference databases used, method and thresholds; as such, the subject is narrower, as it expect only the rank, semantically and syntactically.

Both terms expect the sex of the specimen. However, the object expect the sex for a particular organism of the urobiome, as such, the subject is broader semantically but related syntatically, as the object requires strings from a controlled vocabulary.

Both terms expect the sex of the specimen. However, the object expect the sex for a particular organism of the animal class, as such, the subject is broader semantically but related syntatically, as the object requires strings from a controlled vocabulary.
Both terms expect the sex of the specimen. However, the object expect the sex for a particular organism from the host, as such, the subject is broader semantically but related syntatically.

Both terms expect the sex of the specimen. However, the object expect the sex for a particular organism from the plant class, as such, the subject is broader semantically but related syntatically, as the object requires strings from a controlled vocabulary.

Semantically, both terms expect the type of reproduction, however the subject specifies 'Sexual/asexual/cyclic parthenogen only'. Syntatically, the object recommends values for the field 'Expected_value: for virus: lytic, lysogenic, temperate, obligately lytic; for plasmid: incompatibility group; for eukaryote: asexual, sexual; other more specific values (e.g., incompatibility group) are allowed'.

Both terms expect the life stage, however, the object expects it only for the host, as such, the subject is broader as it does not do that distinction. Syntatically, the object expects only a 'stage', whereas the subject expects a controlled vocabularry ''Adult', 'Immature', 'Juvenile', 'Pupa'.'.

Both terms expect a reference to an external resources related to the specimen. Syntatically, the object expects a structured pattern ('^({PMID}|{DOI}|{URL})$'), where the subject makes no recommendations.

The object expects the date and time of the sampling, either as a single point or as an interval; whereas, the subject specifically expects the start date of the collection, meaning it expects a narrower value. Syntatically, these may be an exact match or a narrow match depending on how the user represents the value.

The object expects the date and time of the sampling, either as a single point or as an interval; whereas, the subject specifically expects the end date of the collection, meaning it expects a narrower value. Syntatically, these may be an exact match or a narrow match depending on how the user represents the value.

The object expects the date and time of the sampling, either as a single point or as an interval; whereas, the subject specifically expects the time of the collection, meaning it expects a narrower value. Syntatically, these may be an exact match or a narrow match depending on how the user represents the value.

The object expects the geographical origin of the sample as defined by the country or sea name followed by specific region name, whereas the subject expects only the country or ocean. The object expects a structured pattern ('^{country}: {region}, {specific_location}$'), where country/ocean would be the first string delimited by a semicolon, whereas the object would expect only the country/ocean name, so it is narrower.

The object expects the geographical origin of the sample as defined by the country or sea name followed by specific region name, whereas the subject expects only the providence or state. The object expects a structured pattern ('^{country}: {region}, {specific_location}$'), where region would be the second string delimited by a space, whereas the object would expect only the providence/state, so it is narrower. Here region from the object could directly or indirectly point to providence/state as a region.

The object expects the geographical origin of the sample as defined by the country or sea name followed by specific region name, whereas the subject expects only the region name. The object expects a structured pattern ('^{country}: {region}, {specific_location}$'), where region would be the second string delimited by a space, whereas the object would expect only the region name, so it is narrower.

The object expects the geographical origin of the sample as defined by the country or sea name followed by specific region name, whereas the subject expects only the specific site name. The object expects a structured pattern ('^{country}: {region}, {specific_location}$'), where specific location would be the last string delimited by a comma, whereas the object would expect only the specific site name, so it is narrower.

Both terms expect the latitude and longitude in degrees format. Syntatically, the object requires a structured pattern ('^{lat} {lon}$') that seems to align with the subject, however the subject provides no clear example for the strucutre, although its expected format is an array.

Both terms have the same meaning, although the object has some ambiguity, allowing for a reference point other than the average sea level to be used. Syntatically, the subject is more narrow than the object as it expects the value in meters, whereas the object allows text to be added to explicit the convention used.

Both terms have the same meaning, although the object has some ambiguity, allowing for a reference point other than the average sea level to be used. Syntatically, the subject is more narrow than the object as it expects the value in meters, whereas the object allows text to be added to explicit the convention used.
The object can include the names, references or descriptions of the methods used to collect a sample; similarly, the subject too with an expected structured pattern.

The subject expects only the sequence for the forward primer (narrower), while the object expects both the forward and reverse primer (broader). Syntatically, the subject expects a single nucleotide sequence per field, whereas the object defines a structured regular expression requiring both forward and reverse primer sequences to be provided within a single field, each prefixed by an identifier (e.g. ‘FWD:’ and ‘REV:’) and separated by a delimiter.

The subject expects only the sequence for the forward primer (narrower), while the object expects both the forward and reverse primer (broader). Syntatically, the subject expects a single nucleotide sequence per field, whereas the object defines a structured regular expression requiring both forward and reverse primer sequences to be provided within a single field, each prefixed by an identifier (e.g. ‘FWD:’ and ‘REV:’) and separated by a delimiter.

The subject expect the biome as inferred through the collection coordinates, whereas teh object expects the broad-scale environmental context using subclasses of EnvO s biome class: http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/ENVO_00000428.

The subject expects the name of the institution with sovereignty over the specimen, whereas the object expects the name of the institution storing the specimen. As such, we expect these to be exact matches semantically. However, syntactically, if the sample is not available, the object expects string 'sample not available', wheras the subject details no requirement.
